title: How to Use Intuition When Dealing with Uncertainty
meta_description: Stop second guessing yourself. Uncertainty is where intuition thrives. Learn to trust the signals you already feel.
og_title: How to Use Intuition When Dealing with Uncertainty
og_description: Stop second guessing yourself. Uncertainty is where intuition thrives. Learn to trust the signals you already feel.
body:
You're Not Broken. You're Just Picking Up the Wrong Signals.

You know that feeling. The one that sits in your gut like a stone. The one that makes your chest tighten when you're staring at a decision with no clear answer. Everyone around you seems fine, but you're spinning. You're asking yourself: "Should I stay? Should I go? Should I say yes? Should I run?" And the worst part? You can't find a single logical reason for the anxiety. It just is.

I'm here to tell you something that might piss you off at first. That feeling isn't your anxiety. It's your intuition. And you've been trained your whole life to ignore it.

Let me explain. When you're dealing with uncertainty - and I mean real, bone-deep uncertainty - your nervous system doesn't just shut down. It opens up. It starts scanning for data that your conscious mind can't process yet. You feel it as a knot in your stomach, a pressure in your head, a sudden urge to leave a room for no reason. That's not fear. That's information.

But here's the problem. You've been told that feelings are unreliable. You've been told that logic is the only way. You've been told that if you can't explain it, it's not real. So when your body starts sending these signals, you interpret them as "something is wrong with me" instead of "something is wrong with this situation." Right?!

"Your intuition isn't the voice that tells you what to do. It's the voice that tells you what's already true. The uncertainty isn't the enemy. The noise in your head is."

So how do you actually use intuition when the ground is shaking under your feet? How do you separate the signal from the static? Let's walk through it step by step. No fluff. No spiritual bypassing. Just practical, grounded work for people who feel too much and have been told they're too sensitive their whole lives.

Step One: Stop Trying to "Fix" the Uncertainty

The first thing you do when uncertainty hits is try to control it. You Google. You ask friends. You make pro-and-con lists. You replay conversations in your head. You try to predict the future. You're basically trying to force the universe to give you a guarantee that doesn't exist.

Here's the hard truth: you can't fix uncertainty. You can only learn to move with it. Intuition doesn't work when you're white-knuckling for control. It works when you relax your grip just enough to let the information flow.

Think of it like this. When you're in a dark room, your eyes don't try to make light. They adjust. They start picking up the faintest glimmers. Your intuition works the same way. The more you force clarity, the more you create static. The more you relax, the more you see what's actually there.

So the first step is counterintuitive: stop trying to get rid of the uncertainty. Accept it. Say to yourself, "I don't know what's going to happen, and that's okay. My body is giving me information right now, and I'm going to listen without trying to solve it."

Does that land? Because if you're like most sensitive people, your instinct is to fix. But fixing the uncertainty isn't your job. Your job is to receive the data.

Step Two: Learn the Difference Between Intuition and Fear

This is the part where most people get stuck. They feel a strong sensation and they assume it's intuition. But here's the thing: fear and intuition can feel almost identical in the body. Both can cause a tight chest, a racing heart, a sinking feeling. So how do you tell them apart?

I use a simple test. Fear is loud, repetitive, and urgent. It screams at you. It tells you the same story over and over. It's obsessed with worst-case scenarios. Intuition, on the other hand, is quiet, steady, and often surprising. It doesn't repeat itself. It shows up once, maybe twice, and then it waits. It doesn't demand action. It just presents information.

Fear says: "If you leave this job, you'll be broke and homeless." Intuition says: "This job is draining you. Something better is coming." Fear is a siren. Intuition is a whisper.

Here's a practical exercise. Next time you're feeling uncertain, sit down and close your eyes. Take three deep breaths. Then ask yourself: "What am I actually afraid of?" Write it down. Then ask: "What do I actually know to be true, beyond the fear?" Write that down too. The first list is your fear. The second list is your intuition - or at least the beginning of it.

You'll notice that fear is always future-oriented. It's always about what might happen. Intuition is present-oriented. It's about what's already happening, right now, in your body and in the situation.

If you want to go deeper on this distinction, I highly recommend checking out Sonia Choquette's "The Answer Is Simple" on Amazon (paid link). She breaks down the difference between the voice of the ego and the voice of the soul in a way that's practical and grounding.

Step Three: Get Out of Your Head and Into Your Body

Your intuition doesn't live in your brain. It lives in your body. That's why you feel it as a sensation, not a thought. So if you want to access it, you have to stop thinking and start sensing.

This is hard for people who pride themselves on being logical. I get it. I used to be one of those people. I thought I could think my way through anything. But uncertainty doesn't respond to thinking. It responds to feeling.

Here's what I want you to try. The next time you're faced with a decision and you don't know what to do, stop. Take a moment to scan your body. Don't judge what you find. Just notice. Where is the tension? Is it in your jaw? Your shoulders? Your stomach? What is the quality of that tension? Is it sharp? Dull? Spreading?

Now, bring your attention to that spot. Don't try to change it. Just breathe into it. And ask: "What is this sensation trying to tell me?" Wait for an answer. It might come as a word, an image, or a feeling. It might not come at all. That's okay. The act of asking is enough.

Over time, you'll start to notice patterns. A tight stomach might mean "no." A sense of expansion in your chest might mean "yes." A dull ache in your shoulders might mean "wait." Your body has its own language. You just have to learn it.

I've been doing this work for years, and I still get surprised. Sometimes my intuition tells me to do something that makes no logical sense. But when I follow it, things work out. Not always in the way I expected, but always in a way that was right for me.

Step Four: Use the "Three Questions" Method

When uncertainty is high, your mind will spin in circles. You'll ask the same questions over and over: "What should I do? What if I'm wrong? What if I miss my chance?" These questions are useless. They're loops, not keys.

Instead, I want you to ask three specific questions. Write them down. Sit with them. Don't try to answer them immediately. Let them simmer.

Question 1: What is the most loving thing I can do for myself right now?

This isn't about being selfish. It's about recognizing that you can't pour from an empty cup. When you're uncertain, your first instinct might be to sacrifice yourself for others. That's fear, not love. The most loving thing might be to say no, to set a boundary, or to take a break.

Question 2: What would I do if I wasn't afraid?

This is the classic question, but it works because it cuts through the noise. Fear is the biggest blocker of intuition. When you remove it, even hypothetically, you get a clearer signal. The answer to this question is usually your intuition speaking.

Question 3: What is the smallest, most immediate step I can take?

Intuition doesn't ask you to leap into the unknown. It asks you to take one step. Then another. Uncertainty feels overwhelming because you're looking at the whole mountain. But your intuition only shows you the next foothold. Trust that.

I often recommend keeping a journal for these questions. Write down the answers, even if they feel silly. Over time, you'll see patterns. You'll start to trust the voice that emerges.

For a more structured approach to this kind of inner work, Gabrielle Bernstein's "The Universe Has Your Back" on Amazon (paid link) is a great resource. She combines practical exercises with a spiritual perspective that's grounded and accessible.

Step Five: Learn to Sit with Not Knowing

This is the hardest step. I'm not going to sugarcoat it. Most of us hate not knowing. We want answers. We want closure. We want the pain to stop. But intuition doesn't work on a timeline. It reveals itself when it's ready, not when you're ready.

Sitting with uncertainty is a skill. It's like building a muscle. The first time you try, it's going to hurt. You're going to want to grab for the nearest answer, even if it's wrong. But if you can stay with the discomfort, something shifts.

Here's what I do. When I feel the urge to force an answer, I say to myself: "I don't know yet, and that's okay. I trust that clarity will come when it's time." Then I take a walk. I do some dishes. I let my hands be busy while my mind rests. And almost always, the answer comes when I'm not looking for it.

This is counterintuitive for people who are used to pushing. But the more you push, the more you block the signal. Intuition is like a shy friend. It shows up when you stop demanding it to perform.

One of the best books I've read on this topic is "Playing Big" by Tara Mohr on Amazon (paid link). She talks about the "inner critic" and the "inner mentor" - and how to tell the difference. It's a breakthrough for anyone who struggles with uncertainty and self-doubt.

Step Six: Take Imperfect Action

Here's the secret that nobody tells you. Intuition isn't about getting it right. It's about moving forward. Even if you take the "wrong" step, you'll learn something. And that learning will guide your next step.

Waiting for perfect clarity is a trap. It's a form of avoidance. You can't know everything before you act. That's not how life works. So take the best step you can, based on the information you have, and trust that the next step will reveal itself.

I call this "imperfect action." It's the opposite of paralysis. It's the willingness to be wrong, to learn, and to course-correct. Intuition loves imperfect action because it creates momentum. And momentum is what breaks the grip of uncertainty.

So here's my challenge to you. Pick one decision you're sitting on. Just one. Don't try to solve the whole thing. Just take the smallest possible action that moves you in the direction of your intuition. Send the email. Make the phone call. Say the thing you've been holding back. Then see what happens.

You don't have to know the whole path. You just have to take the next step.

Step Seven: Trust the Process

The last step is the hardest for people who have been burned before. You've trusted your gut and been wrong. You've followed a feeling and ended up in a worse place. I get it. I've been there too. But here's what I've learned: the failure wasn't in trusting your intuition. It was in how you interpreted the signal.

Intuition is never wrong. But your interpretation of it can be. You might feel a sense of urgency and think it means "run," when actually it means "pay attention." You might feel a sense of dread and think it means "bad," when actually it means "this is going to change you." The signal is always accurate. The meaning is what you have to learn to decode.

So give yourself permission to be a beginner. You're learning a new language. You're going to make mistakes. But every mistake teaches you something. Every wrong turn brings you closer to the right one.

And here's the beautiful thing. The more you practice, the clearer the signal gets. Your body starts to trust you. Your intuition starts to speak louder. And uncertainty? It stops being terrifying. It becomes interesting. It becomes a doorway instead of a wall.

You're Not Lost. You're Just Learning.

If you've made it this far, you're probably the kind of person who feels things deeply. You've been told your whole life that you're too sensitive, too emotional, too much. But I'm telling you the opposite. Your sensitivity is your superpower. It's the thing that allows you to pick up on signals that other people miss. It's the thing that guides you through the dark.

Uncertainty is not your enemy. It's the classroom. And your intuition? It's the teacher. All you have to do is show up, listen, and trust that the answers will come when you're ready for them.

So take a breath. Put your hand on your heart. And say this out loud: "I trust myself. I trust my body. I trust the process." Then go take that next step. Imperfect. Messy. Real.

You've got this. And I'm right here with you.

If you're ready to go deeper and start trusting yourself on a whole new level, take the free assessment at intuitivesawaken.com/assessment to discover your intuitive type and get a personalized plan for navigating uncertainty with clarity and confidence.
